hook: Morocco's archaeology museum, home to the bronze busts dug from Roman Volubilis
facts: Open since 1932 as Morocco's national archaeology museum, on Avenue Moulay Abdelaziz.
Holds about twenty large bronzes excavated at Volubilis, including the bust of Amazigh king Juba II.
Star pieces include the Ephebe pourer and the taut, lunging Dog of Volubilis.
Artefacts were transferred here from Volubilis and Chellah after a 1930 decision to centralise finds.
w: Walk into the oval room and the lost Roman west stares back: twenty-odd bronzes pulled from the dirt at Volubilis, among them the brooding bust of Juba II, the Berber king Rome raised and married off. Nearby a bronze dog crouches mid-lunge, all nerves and tension. Empire, melted down and dug up again.

hook: The central bank's money museum, 30,000 coins from antiquity to the dirham
facts: Inaugurated June 2002 inside the central bank at the corner of Avenue Allal Ben Abdellah and Rue Al-Qahira.
The collection runs to more than 30,000 coins, tokens and banknotes, begun by the bank in 1947.
A chronological hall traces Moroccan money from ancient mintings to the present.
An attached art gallery shows around eighty works from the bank's holdings.
w: Behind the central bank's stern facade, three floors of money: thirty thousand coins, tokens and notes that the bank quietly hoarded from 1947 on. Punic mintings, Idrisid dirhams, sultans' gold, colonial paper, all the way to the cash in your pocket. A history of Morocco told entirely in what it spent.

hook: A blinding-white 1929 Art Deco villa turned contemporary gallery
facts: Built in 1929 as a neocolonial mansion just off Avenue Mohammed V.
Restored for the ONA Foundation by architect Mustapha Alaoui and opened as a gallery in December 2006.
Holds more than 600 works and runs free contemporary exhibitions, mostly Moroccan artists.
It followed the foundation's first Villa des Arts in Casablanca.
w: A 1929 mansion scrubbed back to dazzling Deco white, its curves and grilles half European, half Moroccan craft. The mining conglomerate ONA restored it and threw the doors open free in 2006; inside, six hundred works and rotating shows of Moroccan art. Colonial money's elegant villa, now hung with what came after.

hook: A 1980s royal mosque of fountains, gardens and a retractable roof
facts: Built in the 1980s by King Hassan II and named for his granddaughter, in the embassy quarter of Souissi.
Laid out on a four-iwan plan around an arcaded courtyard, holding around 1,000 worshippers.
Computers run a retractable roof over the hall, a heated floor and a sequence of fountains.
Set among manicured gardens; closed to non-Muslims, but the grounds reward a slow circuit.
w: Out among the embassies of Souissi, Hassan II built his granddaughter a mosque and spared nothing: zellij, painted ceilings, a courtyard ringed by fountains, even a roof that slides open and a heated floor, all run by computer. Non-Muslims stay outside, but the gardens and the play of water do most of the talking anyway.

hook: A rounded bastion on the river cliff, named for a Jewish-born marabout
facts: Stands at the eastern, riverside end of Rabat's early-17th-century Andalusian wall, guarding the old port.
The only tower on that wall with a rounded profile, pierced with loopholes and cannon embrasures.
Bombarded by the English squadron of Admiral Rainsborough in 1637 but kept its shape.
Named for Sidi Makhlouf, a local marabout of Jewish origin, whose tomb adjoins it.
legend: They say the saint Sidi Makhlouf, a convert revered by Muslims and remembered as Jewish-born, watches the river mouth from his adjoining tomb.
w: Where the Andalusian wall meets the Bouregreg cliff, one bastion breaks the pattern: round instead of square, loopholed for cannon, raised by Morisco refugees to guard the port. English ships shelled it in 1637 and it shrugged. It carries the name of Sidi Makhlouf, a Jewish-born holy man whose tomb leans against the stone.

hook: A 1919 lighthouse raised on a bastion built by an English renegade
facts: Built in 1919 under Sultan Moulay Youssef, 31 metres tall and still an active beacon.
Stands on the platform of the Sirat bastion, raised in 1775-76 above the Atlantic cliff.
That bastion was the work of Ahmed El Inglizi, an English convert who served Sultan Mohammed III.
With the Sqala fort and Dar bastion it once defended the mouth of the Bouregreg.
w: The light went up in 1919, but the cliff under it had teeth long before: the Sirat bastion was thrown up in the 1770s by Ahmed El Inglizi, an English convert turned the sultan's engineer, to dare the Atlantic and any ship coming for the river. Renegade's gun platform below, Moulay Youssef's lamp on top, still warning hulls off the rocks.

hook: The colonial dead of Rabat, with French war graves and a Russian chapel beside
facts: A large civilian cemetery on Avenue Hassan II, near Rabat-Agdal station, holding mostly Europeans.
Military plots contain 1,145 who died in French service between 1911 and 1956.
Nine British Commonwealth burials of the Second World War lie here under CWGC care.
The Russian Orthodox chapel and church stand alongside, tying it to Rabat's emigre community.
w: Rabat's other dead lie out by the Agdal tracks: French settlers, colonial soldiers, a handful of Commonwealth war graves tended from London, and Russians who never went home. Eleven hundred uniformed names from 1911 to 1956, plus the quieter civilian ranks. The empire that built the new town, buried in it.

hook: Africa's oldest open Russian church, built by White emigres in 1932
facts: First stone laid July 1931, consecrated November 1932 by Metropolitan Eulogius.
Raised by Russian emigres who fled the 1917 revolution and settled in Rabat.
Reckoned the oldest functioning Russian Orthodox church not only in Morocco but in Africa.
Repainted throughout in 2011 by icon-painters brought from Moscow; stands at Place Bab Tamesna.
w: A flake of Tsarist Russia stranded on the edge of the medina: officers and exiles who fled the Bolsheviks washed up in Rabat and, by 1932, raised this church to the Resurrection. Said to be the oldest working Russian church anywhere in Africa, repainted by Moscow icon-makers in 2011. The lost white army still sings here.

hook: The garden that keeps the bloodline of the lion extinct in the wild
facts: Opened to the public on its present green-belt site in January 2012, replacing the old Temara zoo.
Its first enclosures housed lions kept in the royal palace, descended from Sultan Mohammed V's private animals.
The Barbary or Atlas lion has been extinct in the wild since around 1942.
DNA work by Oxford and Kent found these lions carry the Barbary line, though not in pure form.
legend: Tribesmen once hunted Atlas lions in the mountains and brought them to the sultan as tribute and proof of loyalty, so the legend of the king's lions goes.
w: Gone from the Atlas since the 1940s, the Barbary lion survives here by a thread of royal blood: mountain men once brought captured lions to the sultan as tribute, and their offspring paced the palace grounds before moving to this park. Oxford and Kent traced the line in their DNA. Not pure, but the last maned ghost of Morocco.
